1            Governance

Governance, as per UNDP, is the system of values, policies, and institutions by which a society manages its economic, political, and
social affairs through interactions among the state, civil society, and private sector, organizing decision-making to achieve mutual
understanding and action. Governance encompasses both formal and informal actors and institutions that participate in making
decisions and carrying out their implementation.
                                           UPSC Previous Year Question

     heavily on the integrity, transparency, and accountability of its governing institutions, including the civil services. (2024)

 3. Good Governance
In the 1992 report entitled “Governance and Development,” the World Bank defined good governance as “the manner in which power
is exercised in the management of a country’s economic and social resources for development.” It ensures transparent, accountable,
and corruption-free service delivery, promoting equal opportunities and inclusive growth, especially for marginalised sections.

Good governance ensures transparency, accountability, and efficiency in public administration. Embracing "minimum government,
maximum governance" streamlines processes, reduces bureaucratic interference, and empowers citizens through effective, responsive
systems, fostering inclusive development and trust in institutions.

2 E-Governance
According to the World Bank, e-Governance refers to the use by government agencies of information technologies such as Wide
Area Networks (WANs), the Internet, and mobile computing that have the ability to transform relations with citizens, businesses,
and other arms of government.

       accountability in governments. What inadequacies hamper the enhancement of these features? (2023)

       integral part of the government”. Discuss (2020)
    3. E-governance is not only about the utilisation of the power of new technology but also much about the
       critical importance of the ‘use value’ of information. Explain. (2018)

3 Citizen's Charter
A Citizen’s Charter is a written commitment by government agencies to deliver specific standards of public services transparently
and efficiently. It is rooted in the principles of good governance and outlines the commitments of government agencies to citizens,
including service standards, timelines, and grievance redress mechanisms. While the Citizen’s Charter is not legally enforceable,
it acts as a critical tool for improving public services through the establishment of clear standards, timeframes, and expectations.
                                          UPSC Previous Year Question
    1. The Citizen Charter has been a landmark initiative in ensuring citizen-centric administration.
       But it is yet to reach its full potential. Identify the factors hindering the realisation of its promise
       and suggest measures to overcome them. (2024)
    2. The Citizens’ Charter is an ideal instrument of organisational transparency and accountability,
       but it has its own limitations. Identify the limitations and suggest measures for greater
       effectiveness of the Citizens’ Charter. (2018)

   Remedies                   defaults.

   Fit All                    generic template.

   Commitments
6. Periodic Evaluation      y Regularly review and revise charters, using independent evaluation and end-user feedback.


 6. Sevottam Model
The Sevottam Model is a quality management framework developed by the Department of Administrative Reforms and
Public Grievances (DARPG) and recommended by the 2nd Administrative Reforms Commission (ARC) to improve public
service delivery in India. It aims to make governance more citizen-centric by addressing the limitations of earlier mechanisms
like the Citizens’ Charter.

                                                 Sevottam Model


     Aspect                                                          Description
 1. Three            1.   Citizen Charter: Establishes clear, measurable service standards and opens channels for citizen
    Modules               feedback, empowering citizens to know their rights and entitlements.
                     2.   Grievance Redressal: Mandates an effective, transparent, and responsive system for handling
                          complaints, ensuring citizens feel heard and satisfied with the process regardless of outcome.
                     3.   Service Delivery Capability: Focuses on building and continuously enhancing organisational
                          capacity through training, process improvement, and resource management to deliver high-quality
                          services efficiently.
 2. Seven Steps      1.   Define services and identify clients to align offerings with citizen needs.
                     2.   Set clear, realistic, and measurable standards and norms for each service.
                     3.   Develop organisational capability via staff training, technology adoption, and resource allocation.
                     4.   Perform to achieve the set standards across all units.
                     5.   Monitor performance regularly to ensure standards are met and gaps are addressed promptly.
                     6.   Evaluate impact independently using citizen feedback and report cards.
                     7.   Ensure continuous improvement by updating processes and standards based on evaluation.
 3. Significance     y Empowers citizens by making service standards, entitlements, and grievance mechanisms
                       transparent and accessible.
                     y Promotes accountability and responsiveness in government organisations through clear benchmarks
                       and regular self-assessment.
                     y Facilitates continuous improvement in service delivery by integrating citizen feedback and
                       independent evaluations.
                     y Acts as a quality benchmark and rating tool, encouraging healthy competition and recognition of
                       excellence among public service providers.
                     y Enhances public trust, satisfaction, and participation by making governance more citizen-centric
                       and ethical.
                     y Supports sustainable and systematic reforms in public administration, adaptable to diverse
                       organisational and regional needs.

The Citizen’s Charter is crucial for transparent and accountable public service delivery, but its success depends on citizen
participation, clear standards, and effective grievance redressal. Adopting frameworks like the Sevottam Model can further empower
citizens and ensure continuous improvement in governance.

4            Role of Civil Services
                   in a Democracy
The Indian Civil Services form the permanent executive responsible for implementing government policies, maintaining law and
order, and ensuring efficient public administration across the country, upholding constitutional values of neutrality and
accountability essential for democratic governance.
As Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel famously called them, they are the "steel frame of India," providing stability and continuity to the
nation’s complex and diverse federal system.
                                         UPSC Previous Year Question
    1. The Doctrine of Democratic Governance makes it necessary that the public perception of the
       integrity and commitment of civil servants becomes absolutely positive. Discuss. (2024).

    2. “Institutional quality is a crucial driver of economic performance”. In this context suggest reforms
       in the Civil Service for strengthening democracy. (2020).

    3. Initially, the Civil Services in India were designed to achieve the goals of neutrality and
       effectiveness, which seem to be lacking in the present context. Do you agree with the view that
       drastic reforms are required in the Civil Services? Comment (2017)

    4. “Traditional bureaucratic structure and culture have hampered the process of socio-economic
       development in India.” Comment. (2016).

    5. Has the Cadre-based Civil Services Organisation been the cause of slow in India? Critically
       examine. (2014).

The Indian Civil Services remain the indispensable pillar of governance, ensuring policy implementation, administrative

To meet emerging challenges and sustain democratic governance, continuous reforms focusing on capacity building, technology

According to the World Bank, NGOs are "private, non-profit organisations that pursue activities to relieve suffering, promote the
interests of the poor, protect the environment, provide basic social services, or undertake community development."
NGOs bridge gaps in government services, mobilise communities, and promote inclusive development in areas like education, health,
poverty alleviation, and gender equality. Constitutionally backed by Article 19(1)(c) and aligned with DPSPs like Articles 38 and
43, they promote participatory democracy and social justice.

to take action and create change." In India, NGOs remain vital for social justice, empowerment, and inclusive development. By adopting

6 Self Help Groups(SHGs)
Self-Help Groups (SHGs) are playing a vital role in India’s socio-economic development by promoting grassroots empowerment
and financial inclusion. On the 78th Independence Day, Prime Minister Modi highlighted the impact of SHGs, noting that 10
crore women have benefited from ₹9 lakh crore in government support, driving economic self-reliance and contributing to social
transformation through initiatives like the "Lakhpati Didi" scheme.

                     (Regulation) Act (FCRA)                                                                        Click or Scan

Foreign Contribution (Regulation) Act (FCRA), enacted in 1976 and amended in 2010 and 2020, governs foreign contributions
to NGOs. Its primary purpose is to ensure that foreign funds are used transparently and responsibly, preventing misuse that could
threaten India’s sovereignty, integrity, and security.

       Foreign Contribution (Regulation) Act (FCRA), 1976. (2015)

    Registration            educational, religious, or social programs.

    Entities                government servants are prohibited from receiving foreign donations.

    Funds                   compliance with FCRA regulations.
 5. Reporting             y NGOs can transfer only to other FCRA-registered NGOs. Complete sub-grant ban introduced in 2020

Foreign Contribution Regulation Act (FCRA) is vital for safeguarding India’s sovereignty by regulating foreign donations to
NGOs and ensuring transparency and accountability in their utilization. While recent amendments have eased compliance and
enhanced operational flexibility, continuous reforms are essential to support genuine organizations effectively.
